sections.heading: 3…) and the sections are divided into paragraphs (numbered 1.1,
sections.title: Legal entity and shareholder protections for small businesses
sections.summary: This section explains that a proprietary company has a separate legal existence distinct from its owners, managers, and employees. It owns property, rights, and obligations, and can enter contracts, sue, and be sued. Shareholders are not liable for company debts beyond unpaid shares, though directors may be personally liable for company debts or breaches of duty. Directors can also be liable for losses if acting as trustees.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the legal separation of a company from its owners, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments as it defines the entity responsible for data processing and the scope of liability in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: A company has a separate legal existence distinct from its owners, managers, employees, and agents
A company has its own property, rights, and obligations
Shareholders are not liable for company debts beyond unpaid shares
Directors may be liable for company debts if the company cannot pay them
Directors may be liable for losses from breaches of duty to the company

sections.source_quote: A company has a separate legal existence that is distinct from that of its owners, managers, operators, employees and agents. A company has its own property, its own rights and its own obligations.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for data entities

sections.heading: or set up new companies themselves.
sections.title: Company registration and basic operational requirements
sections.summary: This section outlines the process for setting up a company, including registration with ASIC, naming requirements, and the use of ACNs. It specifies that a company must have a unique Australian Company Number (ACN), must include 'Proprietary Limited' or 'Pty Ltd' in its name, and must maintain registers of shareholders and directors. Companies can ratify contracts entered before registration.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it defines the legal entity structure and registration process, which impacts data ownership and transfer responsibilities in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: A company must have a unique Australian Company Number (ACN)
A company name must include 'Proprietary Limited' or 'Pty Ltd'
A company must maintain a register of shareholders
A company can ratify contracts entered before registration

sections.source_quote: When a company is registered, ASIC allocates to it a unique 9 digit number called the Australian Company Number (ACN).
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential company registration rules

sections.heading: the Corporations Act are discussed below.
sections.title: Ongoing obligations for registered companies
sections.summary: This section details annual obligations for companies, including the use of company names and ACNs, the submission of extracts of particulars to ASIC, payment of review fees, and notification requirements for changes to company details. Companies must update ASIC within specific timeframes for changes to directors, shareholders, and registered office addresses.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it establishes ongoing compliance obligations that affect data retention, storage, and transfer processes across borders.

requirements.text: A company must display its name at business premises
A company must pay an annual review fee to ASIC
A company must notify ASIC of changes to directors within 28 days
A company must notify ASIC of changes to registered office within 28 days

sections.source_quote: Each year, ASIC issues each company with an extract of particulars within 2 weeks of the company's review date.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential company obligations

sections.heading: 5  Company directors and company secretaries
sections.title: Director and company secretary responsibilities
sections.summary: This section specifies the requirements for directors and company secretaries, including age restrictions, residency requirements, consent procedures, and specific duties such as acting in good faith, avoiding conflicts of interest, and maintaining records. Directors may face criminal penalties for failing to meet their duties, and their obligations continue even after company deregistration.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is critical for DTIAs as it defines the roles and responsibilities of individuals handling data, impacting accountability and data processing practices in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Directors must act in good faith and in the best interests of the company
Directors must avoid conflicts of interest
Directors must exercise care and diligence
Directors must prevent company trading while unable to pay debts

sections.source_quote: In managing the business of a company, each of its directors is subject to a wide range of duties under the Corporations Act and other laws.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance duties not directly data-specific

sections.heading: [sections 461—462]
sections.title: Shareholder registration and share transfer mechanisms
sections.summary: A person may become a shareholder through company registration, share issuance, or purchase from existing shareholders. Shareholders cease to be shareholders by selling all shares, company buyback, or ASIC cancellation. The section outlines specific transfer mechanisms and conditions for share transfers, with directors having discretion to refuse registration.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines share transfer procedures and conditions that may impact cross-border data transfers involving shareholder information, though not directly relevant to data transfers.

requirements.text: A person may become a shareholder of a company in several ways, including being listed in the application for registration of the company
A person may become a shareholder of a company by the company issuing shares to the person
A person may become a shareholder of a company by buying shares from an existing shareholder and having the company register the transfer
A person ceases to be a shareholder by selling all shares and having the company register the transfer
A person ceases to be a shareholder by the company buying back all shares
A person ceases to be a shareholder by ASIC cancelling the company's registration
The directors have a discretion to refuse to register a transfer of shares

sections.source_quote: A person may become a shareholder of a company in several ways, including the following: • the person being listed as a shareholder of the company in the application for registration of the company • the company issuing shares to the person • the person buying shares in the company from an existing shareholder and the company registering the transfer.
sections.relevance_reason: Shareholder registration unrelated to data compliance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer rules

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Types of companies that can be registered
sections.summary: This section specifies the types of companies that can be registered under the Act, including limited by shares, unlimited with share capital, limited by guarantee, no liability companies, and corporate collective investment vehicles. It also outlines conditions for registering a no liability company, such as having share capital, specific constitutional requirements, and restrictions on activities outside mining purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines company types relevant to data processing structures but does not directly impact data transfer mechanisms or international data flows.

requirements.text: A company may be registered as a no liability company only if it has share capital
A company's constitution states that its sole objects are mining purposes
A company has no contractual right to recover calls made on shares from shareholders who fail to pay
A no liability company must not engage in activities outside its mining purposes
A no liability company's directors must not let whole or proportion of a mine on tribute without special resolution approval
A no liability company's directors must not make contracts for working land on tribute without special resolution approval
A company must have no more than 50 non-employee shareholders to be registered as a proprietary company

sections.source_quote: Section 112(2) of the Australian Charities and Not-for-profits Commission Act 2: A company may be registered as a no liability company only if: (a) the company has a share capital; and (b) the company's constitution states that its sole objects are mining purposes; and (c) the company has no contractual right under its constitution to recover calls made on its shares from a shareholder who fails to pay them.
sections.relevance_reason: No data compliance relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Application requirements for company registration
sections.summary: This section details the information required for applying to register a company with ASIC, including company type, name, members, directors, company secretary, registered office address, and share details. It specifies that the application must be in prescribed form and that consents and agreements must be provided by applicants.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines the registration process but does not directly address data transfer mechanisms or international data flows.

requirements.text: The application must state the type of company proposed to be registered
The application must state the company's proposed name (unless ACN is used)
The application must state the name and address of each person who consents to become a member
The application must state the present and former given and family names and date of birth of each person who consents to become a director
The application must state the present and former given and family names and date of birth of each person who consents to become a company secretary
The application must state the address of each person who consents to become a director or company secretary
The application must state the address of the company's proposed registered office
The application must state the address of the company's proposed principal place of business (if different from registered office)

sections.source_quote: Section 117(2) of the Australian Charities and Not-for-profits Commission Act 2: The application must state: (a) the type of company that is proposed to be registered under this Act; (b) the company's proposed name (unless the ACN is to be used in its name); (c) the name and address of each person who consents to become a member; (d) the present given and family name, all former given and family names and the date and place of birth of each person who consents in writing to become a director; (e) the present given and family name, all former given and family names and the date and place of birth of each person who consents in writing to become a company secretary; (f) the address of each person who consents in writing to become a director or company secretary; (g) the address of the company's proposed registered office; (h) for a public company—the proposed opening hours of its registered office (if not standard hours); (i) for a company limited by shares or unlimited company—the number and class of shares each member agrees to take up; (j) the State or Territory in which the company is to be taken to be registered.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure company registration requirements, no data compliance elements

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Assumptions people dealing with companies are entitled to make
sections.summary: This section states that assumptions made under it apply for the purposes of the section itself. It clarifies that public information from ASIC does not constitute constructive notice of a company's existence or status. A person is not deemed to have information about a company merely because such information is publicly available from ASIC.

requirements.text: Assumptions made under this section apply for the purposes of this section

sections.source_quote:
Assumptions people dealing with companies are entitled to make Part 2B.2
Section 130 (8) Without limiting the generality of this section, the assumptions that may be made under this section apply for the purposes of this section.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Table of replaceable rules for company governance
sections.summary: This section provides a table listing replaceable rules applicable to companies, including provisions related to directors, meetings, and shares. These rules govern internal management and have effect as contracts between the company and its members, directors, and other members.

requirements.text: Replaceable rules apply to companies as specified in the table

sections.source_quote: Section 141 (1) The following table sets out the provisions of this Act that apply as replaceable rules.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure company governance rules, no data compliance relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Company name requirements and name change procedures
sections.summary: Companies must use available names or 'Australian Company Number' followed by their ACN. Limited companies must include 'Limited' or 'Proprietary Limited' in their name. Unlimited proprietary companies must include 'Proprietary'. No liability companies must include 'No Liability'. Public companies may not include 'Proprietary' unless they were public companies before 1 July 1998. Companies must notify ASIC if they cease to meet the conditions for omitting 'Limited' from their name. Name changes require special resolutions and ASIC applications, with specific rules for companies under administration or liquidation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs company name requirements and name changes, which may impact data transfer if company names are used in international documents or data processing activities. Name changes require ASIC approval and may affect data residency and cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: A company may have as its name an available name or the expression 'Australian Company Number' followed by the company's ACN
A limited public company must have the word 'Limited' at the end of its name unless section 150 or 151 applies
A limited proprietary company must have the words 'Proprietary Limited' at the end of its name
An unlimited proprietary company must have the word 'Proprietary' at the end of its name
A no liability company must have the words 'No Liability' at the end of its name
A public company must not include the word 'Proprietary' (or an abbreviation) in its name unless it was a public company before 1 July 1998 and included 'Proprietary' in its name before 1 July 1998
A company that does not have 'Limited' at the end of its name must notify ASIC if it ceases to be registered as a charity or if its constitution changes

A company may omit 'Limited' from its name if it is registered as a charity under the Australian Charities and Not-for-profits Commission Act 2012 and its constitution prohibits paying fees to directors
A company may omit 'Limited' from its name if it had a licence that allowed it to do so before 1 July 1998
A company must pass a special resolution adopting a new name and lodge an application with ASIC to change its name
A company must lodge a copy of the special resolution with ASIC within 14 days after it is passed
ASIC must change the company's name by altering the details of the company's registration to reflect the change if the proposed name is available

sections.source_quote: Section 148: A company may have as its name: (a) an available name; or (b) the expression 'Australian Company Number' followed by the company's ACN.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure company registration rules, no data handling

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer rules

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Company type conversion requirements and applications
sections.summary: This section outlines the process for companies to change their legal structure, including specific requirements for applications to ASIC. It mandates that companies lodge applications with ASIC, include special resolutions, and provide detailed information about share changes. The section also specifies conditions under which companies may convert to different types, such as proprietary companies or public companies, and requires compliance with certain constitutional provisions and share capital rules.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it details the requirements for company type conversions, which may impact data storage and processing structures across borders if the company operates internationally. The conversion process involves specific documentation and regulatory compliance that could affect data handling.

requirements.text: A company must lodge an application with ASIC to change its type
The application must include a copy of the special resolution resolving to change the type
For companies limited by guarantee changing to companies limited by shares, the application must include a statement signed by directors that creditors are not likely to be materially prejudiced
For companies limited by shares or companies limited by guarantee changing to unlimited companies, the application must include an assent to the change signed by all members
For proprietary companies changing to public companies, the application must include a consolidated copy of the constitution
The company must have consents from persons to whom shares will be issued when changing from a company limited by guarantee to a company limited by shares
ASIC must give notice to creditors if a company limited by guarantee is changing to a company limited by shares
The company must lodge a copy of the special resolution with ASIC within 14 days after it is passed

sections.source_quote: Section 163(1): To change its type, a company must lodge an application with ASIC.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Mutual capital instruments and MCI mutual entities
sections.summary: This section defines mutual capital instruments (MCIs) and MCI mutual entities, specifying requirements for their issuance and operation. It outlines the conditions under which a mutual entity can issue MCIs, including constitutional provisions and voting rights. The section also details the special procedures for amending constitutions to allow MCI issuance and the requirements for MCI cancellation during demutualization.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it establishes specific data structures and governance requirements for mutual entities issuing MCIs, which could impact data storage and processing across borders. The MCI framework may affect how data is handled in international contexts.

requirements.text: An MCI mutual entity must be a public company
An MCI mutual entity must not have voting shares quoted on a declared financial market
An MCI mutual entity must not be a registered entity within the meaning of the Australian Charities and Not-for-profits Commission Act 2012
An MCI mutual entity's constitution must state it is intended to be an MCI mutual entity
An MCI must be a share in a mutual entity that meets voting rights and other requirements
MCIs must be fully paid shares
Dividends for MCIs must be non-cumulative
MCIs must be cancelled before the entity demutualizes

sections.source_quote: Section 167AC: A mutual entity is an MCI mutual entity if: (a) the entity is a public company; (b) the entity does not have voting shares (other than MCIs) quoted on a declared financial market; ...
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics and company structure

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Registration of members and option holders for companies and schemes
sections.summary: This section mandates that companies and registered schemes maintain specific registers, including a register of members, option holders, and debenture holders. The registers must contain detailed information about members, such as names, addresses, share details, and dates of entry. For companies with more than 50 members, an index of members' names must be maintained. The registers must be updated for transfers and changes, and the company must notify ASIC of changes within specified timeframes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for maintaining detailed member and option registers, which are critical for data storage compliance. It specifies data retention periods, update procedures, and notification obligations that impact cross-border data transfers involving member information.

requirements.text: A company or registered scheme must set up and maintain a register of members
The register must contain the name, address, and date of entry for each member
For companies with more than 50 members, an index of members' names must be maintained
The register must show share details including allotment dates, share numbers, and class of shares
The register must indicate whether shares are held beneficially or non-beneficially
The register must show the amount paid on shares and whether shares are fully paid
The register must be updated whenever options are exercised or expire
The company must notify ASIC within 7 days of changes to the register

sections.source_quote: Section 167A(1) and (2) and Section 169(1) to (8)
sections.relevance_reason: Record keeping for members' data

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer rules

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Notification of member register changes to ASIC within specified timeframes
sections.summary: Proprietary companies must notify ASIC within specific timeframes when they add or alter entries in their member register following certain events, including court orders for corrections, share divisions, share conversions, share issuances, or share capital reductions. The notification deadlines are tied to the company's existing notification obligations under other sections of the Corporations Act. For events not covered by the listed items, companies have 28 days to notify ASIC after making the change.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes time-bound notification requirements for member register updates, which could impact data transfer impact assessments when cross-border transfers involve corporate member registers or shareholding data.

requirements.text: Company must notify ASIC within time frame for correction under subsection 175(3) when court orders correction of member register
Company must notify ASIC within time frame for share division or conversion under subsection 246F(1) when dividing shares into classes or converting shares
Company must notify ASIC within time frame for share issue under subsection 254X(1) when issuing shares
Company must notify ASIC within time frame for share capital reduction under subsection 256C(3) when reducing share capital
Company must notify ASIC within 28 days after adding or altering member register entries for events not covered by items 1-4

sections.source_quote: The company must notify ASIC within this time... 1 the Court orders the company to correct its member register kept under section 169 at the same time that it notifies ASIC of the correction under subsection 175(3)
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: company governance, not direct data compliance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Corporate directors' and secretaries' responsibilities for compliance with corporate obligations
sections.summary: Directors and secretaries of proprietary companies are personally liable for corporate contraventions of specific provisions related to registered offices, share structures, and other corporate responsibilities. This includes obligations to ensure compliance with sections on registered office accessibility, share changes, and annual reporting. Directors must also demonstrate they took reasonable steps to ensure compliance with these provisions to avoid liability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates personal liability for directors and secretaries when corporate obligations are not met, which could affect data transfer impact assessments involving corporate data sharing or cross-border transfers of corporate information.

requirements.text: Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 142 (registered office)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 145 (public company's registered office to be open to public)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 146 (change of principal place of business)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 178A (change to proprietary company's member register)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 178C (change to proprietary company's share structure)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes subsection 203AA(6) (notification of resignation day)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 205B (lodgement of notices with ASIC)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 254X (issue of shares)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 319 (lodgement of annual reports with ASIC)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 320 (lodgement of half-year reports with ASIC)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 346C (response to extract of particulars)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 348D (response to return of particulars)
Secretary of a company contravenes if company contravenes section 349A (change to proprietary company's ultimate holding company)

sections.source_quote: A secretary of a company contravenes this subsection if the company contravenes any of the following provisions (each of which is a corporate responsibility provision)
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Director disclosure of personal interests in company matters
sections.summary: Directors must disclose material personal interests in company matters to other directors. The disclosure must be made in writing to individual directors if given to directors, and the nature and extent of the interest must be recorded in meeting minutes. The standing notice takes effect immediately upon disclosure and ceases to have effect if a new director with no prior interest is appointed. If the interest materially increases, the standing notice ceases to apply. This section applies to directors of proprietary companies and public companies, with specific rules for voting and transactions involving conflicts of interest.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes disclosure requirements for directors with material personal interests in company matters, which could impact data transfer assessments when directors handle sensitive financial or personal information across borders.

requirements.text: Standing notice must be given to other directors individually in writing
Standing notice must be tabled at next directors' meeting after being given
Nature and extent of interest disclosed in standing notice must be recorded in meeting minutes
Standing notice takes effect immediately upon disclosure
Standing notice ceases to have effect if a new director with no prior interest is appointed
Standing notice reactivates if new director with interest is appointed
Standing notice ceases to apply if interest materially increases

sections.source_quote: Section 192(3): If the standing notice is given to the other directors individually in writing, it must be tabled at the next directors' meeting after it is given.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for director disclosures

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Directors' powers to delegate authority and access company records
sections.summary: Directors may delegate powers to committees, directors, employees, or other persons, with delegation recorded in minutes. Directors have the right to inspect company books (excluding financial records) for legal proceedings, and this right extends for seven years after ceasing to be a director. The delegation must be recorded in the company's minute book and the delegate must exercise powers according to directors' directions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs directors' authority delegation and access to company records, which could affect data transfer assessments when sensitive financial or personal information is handled across borders.

requirements.text: Directors may delegate powers to committees, directors, employees, or other persons
Delegation must be recorded in company's minute book
Delegate must exercise powers according to directors' directions
Director may inspect company books (excluding financial records) for legal proceedings
Right to inspect books continues for 7 years after ceasing to be director

sections.source_quote: Section 198E(1): The director of a proprietary company who is its only director and only shareholder may exercise all the powers of the company except any powers that this Act or the company's constitution (if any) requires the company to exercise in general meeting.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for data access

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Indemnity and insurance restrictions for officers and auditors
sections.summary: A company cannot indemnify a person against legal costs incurred in defending actions where the person is found liable for which they could not be indemnified under section 199B(2), in criminal proceedings, or in proceedings brought by ASIC or a liquidator for court orders. The outcome of proceedings includes any related appeals. This applies to legal costs in specific contexts such as disqualification, oppression, or civil penalties.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts indemnification of officers/auditors against legal costs in certain cross-border or regulatory proceedings, impacting data transfer where legal costs are involved in international disputes.

requirements.text: A company must not indemnify a person against legal costs incurred in defending actions where the person is found liable for which they could not be indemnified under subsection (2)
A company must not indemnify a person against legal costs incurred in defending criminal proceedings where the person is found guilty
A company must not indemnify a person against legal costs incurred in defending proceedings brought by ASIC or a liquidator for court orders

sections.source_quote: When indemnity for legal costs not allowed (3) A company or related body corporate must not indemnify a person against legal costs incurred in defending an action for a liability incurred as an officer or auditor of the company if the costs are incurred: (a) in defending or resisting proceedings in which the person is found to have a liability for which they could not be indemnified under subsection (2); or (b) in defending or resisting criminal proceedings in which the person is found guilty; or (c) in defending or resisting proceedings brought by ASIC or a liquidator for a court order if the grounds for making the order are found by the court to have been established
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for legal indemnity

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer rules

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Pension and lump sum payment calculations for retirement benefits
sections.summary: This section defines the calculation method for pension and lump sum payments upon retirement from a company or related body corporate. It specifies that the value of benefits must not exceed the amount calculated based on the relevant period, which is determined by the duration the person held managerial or executive office. The calculation varies depending on the length of the relevant period (less than 1 year, 1-2 years, 2-3 years, or 3+ years) and involves estimating annual base salary. The section also requires disregarding certain contributions when determining the value of payments.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies how to calculate pension and lump sum payments for retirement, which affects cross-border data transfers involving financial data of employees. The calculation method must be applied consistently to ensure compliance with retirement benefit structures.

requirements.text: The relevant period for a person holding managerial or executive office is the period they held that office throughout a single period or multiple periods.
For pension and lump sum payments, the value must not exceed the amount calculated under subsection (2) or (3) based on the relevant period.
The amount calculated for a relevant period less than 1 year is a reasonable estimate of the base salary for 1 year.
The amount calculated for a relevant period of 1-2 years is the average annual base salary worked out as if the period were 2 years.
The amount calculated for a relevant period of 2-3 years is the average annual base salary worked out as if the period were 3 years.
The amount calculated for a relevant period of 3+ years is the average annual base salary from the last 3 years.
When determining the value of a pension or lump sum payment, disregard contributions made by the person or other entities.

sections.source_quote: Section 200G: (1) Subsection 200B(1) does not apply to a benefit if: (a) the benefit is a payment in connection with a person's retirement from an office or position in a company or a related body corporate; and (b) the payment is for past services the person rendered to: (i) the company; or (ii) a related body corporate; or (iii) a body that was a related body corporate of the company when the past services were rendered; and (c) the value of the benefit, when added to the value of all other benefits (if any) already given in connection with the person's retirement from offices or positions in the company and related bodies corporate does not exceed the amount worked out under whichever of subsections (2) and (3) is applicable.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial calculation rules, no data protection link

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Director appointments and confirmations for proprietary and public companies
sections.summary: This section outlines the rules for appointing directors, including the requirement for proprietary companies to confirm appointments within 2 months and public companies to confirm appointments at the next general meeting. It also covers the appointment of alternate directors, managing directors, and the conditions under which appointments are valid. The section specifies that directors may appoint themselves as managing directors and that appointments must be in writing with copies given to the company.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs director appointments and confirmations, which impacts data transfers involving employee information and company governance data. The rules ensure that director appointments are properly documented and confirmed, affecting data storage and processing practices.

requirements.text: A company may appoint a person as a director by resolution passed in general meeting.
If a person is appointed as a director of a proprietary company, the company must confirm the appointment by resolution within 2 months.
If a person is appointed as a director of a public company, the company must confirm the appointment by resolution at the next general meeting.
The directors may appoint 1 or more of themselves to the office of managing director for the period and on terms as they see fit.
With the other directors' approval, a director may appoint an alternate to exercise some or all of their powers for a specified period.
The appointing director may terminate the alternate's appointment at any time.
An appointment or its termination must be in writing and a copy must be given to the company.

sections.source_quote: Section 201G: A company may appoint a person as a director by resolution passed in general meeting.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Director resignation and compensation mechanisms
sections.summary: This section establishes that a company may compensate a director if the board limit prevents the company from appointing them as a director. The suffering party (company or candidate) can seek court remedies for losses caused by the board limit contravention. The contravention does not constitute an offence, and the court may order compensation under section 1325.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses potential financial compensation claims arising from board limit restrictions, which could impact data transfer impact assessments when directors' data is affected by appointment limitations.

requirements.text: The company may compensate a director if the board limit prevents the company from appointing them as a director
The suffering party may institute a court proceeding for contravention of the board limit
The contravention does not constitute an offence

sections.source_quote: Section 201U(5) and (6): The suffering party may institute a proceeding in the Court for the contravention. Contravention does not give rise to an offence
sections.relevance_reason: irrelevant: company governance, not data compliance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Director resignation procedures and effectiveness
sections.summary: This section outlines the procedures for directors to resign, including the effective date of resignation. Resignation takes effect on the day the person stops being a director unless a court or ASIC fixes a different date. The resignation is void if the company has no directors after the resignation takes effect. The company must notify ASIC of resignation within 28 days.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts data transfer impact assessments by defining the effective date of director resignation, which affects data ownership and responsibility periods for personal information.

requirements.text: A director may resign by giving written notice to the company
Resignation takes effect on the day the person stops being a director unless ASIC or the Court fixes a different date
The resignation is void if the company has no directors after the resignation takes effect
The company must notify ASIC of resignation within 28 days

sections.source_quote: Section 203AA(1): A person's resignation as a director takes effect on the day they stopped being a director unless ASIC or the Court fixes a different date
sections.relevance_reason: irrelevant: company governance, not data compliance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Alternative address for service in legal notices
sections.summary: This section specifies conditions under which a person may provide an alternative address for service of legal notices. The address must be in Australia, must be a valid address for service, and a person may have only one such address at any time. The person must notify ASIC of their usual residential address before using the alternative address and must update ASIC within 14 days of any change. ASIC may disclose the address to courts to enforce judgments.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for alternative service addresses that could impact cross-border data transfers if addresses are used for international legal notices.

requirements.text: Alternative address must be in Australia and be one at which documents can be served on the person
A person is entitled to have only 1 alternative address under this section
Before using the alternative address, must lodge with ASIC notice of usual residential address
Within 14 days of any change in usual residential address, must lodge with ASIC notice of the change
ASIC may disclose the person's usual residential address to a court officer for enforcing judgments

sections.source_quote: Section 205E(2) - A person who takes advantage of subsection (2) must: (a) before or at the same time as the alternative address is first included in a notice or application, lodge with ASIC notice of the person's usual residential address; and (b) lodge with ASIC notice of any change in the person's usual residential address within 14 days after the change.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Disqualification for repeated contraventions
sections.summary: This section provides for disqualification of individuals who have repeatedly failed to prevent contraventions of the Act while acting as officers of corporations, including at least two instances of contraventions or failure to take reasonable steps to prevent contraventions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes mechanisms for disqualification based on repeated contraventions that could impact data transfers involving individuals with a history of regulatory breaches.

requirements.text: Person becomes disqualified from managing corporations if they have at least twice been an officer of a body corporate that has contravened this Act and failed to take reasonable steps to prevent contravention
Person becomes disqualified from managing corporations if they have at least twice contravened this Act or the Corporations Act while being an officer of a body corporate

Person becomes disqualified from managing corporations if they have been an officer of a body corporate and done something that would have contravened subsection 180(1) or section 181 if the body corporate had been a corporation

sections.source_quote: Section 206E(1) - (a) the person: (i) has at least twice been an officer of a body corporate that has contravened this Act or the Corporations Act they were an officer of the body corporate and each time the person has failed to take reasonable steps to prevent the contravention; or (ii) has at least twice contravened this Act or the Corporations Act while they were an officer of a body corporate; or (iii) has been an officer of a body corporate and has done something that would have contravened subsection 180(1) or section 181 if the body corporate had been a corporation.
sections.relevance_reason: Disqualification for repeated contraventions

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Disqualification of corporate officers under competition and consumer laws
sections.summary: A person is disqualified from managing corporations if a court order under the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 or ASIC Act is in force. ASIC may disqualify a person for up to 5 years if they were an officer of multiple corporations that wound up within 7 years, with specific conditions on financial recoveries and employee entitlements. The disqualification takes effect immediately after notice is served and may be subject to court review for leave to manage corporations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for disqualifying corporate officers, which could impact data transfer if disqualified individuals handle sensitive financial data across borders.

requirements.text: A person is disqualified from managing corporations if a court order disqualifying the person from managing corporations is in force under section 86E of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010

A person is disqualified from managing corporations if a court order disqualifying the person from managing corporations is in force under section 248 of Schedule 2 to the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
ASIC may disqualify a person from managing corporations for up to 5 years if within 7 years before notice, the person was an officer of 2 or more corporations that wound up
ASIC must give the person a notice in prescribed form requiring them to demonstrate why they should not be disqualified
ASIC must give the person an opportunity to be heard on the question
ASIC must be satisfied that disqualification is justified
ASIC must serve a notice on the person advising them of disqualification in prescribed form
Disqualification takes effect from the time when notice is served on the person

sections.source_quote: Section 206EA: A person is disqualified from managing corporations if a court order disqualifying the person from managing corporations is in force under: (a) section 86E of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010; or (b) section 248 of Schedule 2 to that Act, as that section applies as a law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Ban on hedging remuneration of key management personnel
sections.summary: Key management personnel for disclosing entities cannot enter into arrangements that limit their exposure to risk related to remuneration that has not vested or remains subject to a holding lock. Remuneration not payable until a specific date is considered unvested until that date. Contravention is an offence of strict liability, with additional offences for closely related parties and reckless conduct.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts financial arrangements affecting key personnel remuneration, which could impact data transfer if personnel handle sensitive financial data across borders.

requirements.text: A member of key management personnel for a disclosing entity must not enter into arrangements limiting exposure to risk relating to remuneration that has not vested or remains subject to a holding lock
Remuneration not payable until a specific date is considered unvested until that date
Contravention of subsection (1) is an offence of strict liability
A closely related party of a key management personnel commits an offence if they intentionally contravene subsection (1)

sections.source_quote: Section 206J: A member of the key management personnel for a company that is a disclosing entity, or a closely related party of such a member, must not enter into an arrangement (with anyone) if the arrangement would have the effect of limiting the exposure of the member to risk relating to an element of the member's remuneration that: (a) has not vested in the member; or (b) has vested in the member but remains subject to a holding lock.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial regulation, no data protection link

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Exceptions to member approval for financial benefits to related parties
sections.summary: This section outlines exceptions where member approval is not required for financial benefits to related parties. It specifies that benefits like indemnities, exemptions, insurance premiums, and legal costs for officers are exempt if they are reasonable and given to related parties who are officers. It also includes thresholds for small amounts and benefits to closely-held subsidiaries without discrimination.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides exceptions for financial transfers to related parties without requiring member approval, relevant for DTIAs when assessing whether cross-border transfers to related entities need additional safeguards.

requirements.text: Member approval not needed for indemnities, exemptions, or insurance premiums for officers if reasonable
Member approval not needed for legal costs payments if reasonable and section 199A does not apply
Total small financial benefits must be less than or equal to the prescribed amount
Benefits to closely-held subsidiaries do not require member approval
Benefits must not discriminate unfairly against other members
Court orders do not require member approval for financial benefits

sections.source_quote: Member approval is not needed to give a financial benefit if: (a) the benefit is for a related party who is an officer of the public company or entity; and (b) the benefit is an indemnity, exemption or insurance premium in respect of a liability incurred as an officer of the public company or entity; and (c) to give the benefit would be reasonable in the circumstances of the public company or entity giving the benefit.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial benefits exceptions for related parties

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: data transfer rules

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Membership and oppressive conduct of company affairs
sections.summary: A person is a member of a company if registered on the members' register or becomes a member through conversion from a guarantee company. The court may order remedies for oppressive conduct affecting members, including winding up the company, modifying its constitution, or purchasing shares. The court considers whether conduct is contrary to members' interests or unfairly prejudicial to individual members. A person who inherits shares by will or operation of law is treated as a member for these purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which courts can intervene in company affairs to prevent oppressive conduct, relevant to data transfers involving company members or third parties.

requirements.text: A person is a member of a company if they are registered on the members' register or become a member through conversion from a guarantee company
The court may order winding up the company if conduct is oppressive to members
The court may modify or repeal the company's constitution to address oppressive conduct
The court may order the purchase of shares by members or persons who inherit shares
The court may order the purchase of shares with a reduction in the company's share capital
The court may authorize members or inheritors to institute proceedings on behalf of the company
The court may appoint a receiver or receiver and manager for company property
The court may restrain a person from engaging in specified conduct
The court may require a person to do a specified act
A person who inherits shares by will or operation of law is treated as a member

sections.source_quote: Section 231: A person is a member of a company if they: (a) are a member of the company on its registration; or (b) agree to become a member of the company after its registration and their name is entered on the register of members; or (c) become a member of the company under section 167 (membership arising from conversion of a company from one limited by guarantee to one limited by shares).
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: company governance, not data compliance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer rules

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Company must lodge share division or conversion documents with ASIC
sections.summary: A company must lodge with ASIC a notice in prescribed form when dividing shares into classes or converting shares between classes. The notice must be lodged within 14 days after the division or conversion. Public companies must also lodge copies of documents that attach, vary or cancel share rights or bind classes of members. The company must send members copies of documents within 7 days after payment or approval by ASIC.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires companies to notify ASIC about share class changes, which may impact data transfer if share structures affect data classification or ownership.

requirements.text: A company must lodge with ASIC a notice in prescribed form when dividing shares into classes or converting shares between classes
The notice must be lodged within 14 days after the division or conversion
Public companies must lodge copies of documents that attach, vary or cancel share rights or bind classes of members
A company must send members copies of documents within 7 days after payment or approval by ASIC

sections.source_quote: Section 246F(1): A company must lodge with ASIC a notice in the prescribed form setting out particulars of any of the following: (a) a division of shares in the company into classes if the shares were not previously so divided; (b) a conversion of shares in a class of shares in the company into shares in another class.
sections.relevance_reason: Share division/ASIC compliance unrelated to data protection

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Members' right to request company to distribute statements
sections.summary: Members may request a company to distribute statements about resolutions or other matters to all members. The request must be in writing and signed by at least 100 members entitled to vote. The company must distribute the statement within 7 days after receipt, unless the statement is over 1,000 words or defamatory. Members are liable for distribution costs if the company does not receive the request in time.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows members to distribute statements about resolutions, which could affect data sharing if statements contain personal information.

requirements.text: Members may request a company to give to all its members a statement provided by the members making the request about a resolution or other matter
The request must be in writing and signed by at least 100 members entitled to vote
The company must distribute the statement within 7 days after receipt
The company need not comply if the statement is over 1,000 words or defamatory

sections.source_quote: Section 249P(1): Members may request a company to give to all its members a statement provided by the members making the request about: (a) a resolution that is proposed to be moved at a general meeting; or (b) any other matter that may be properly considered at a general meeting.
sections.relevance_reason: pure company governance, no data compliance relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Member participation in meetings: physical and virtual venue requirements
sections.summary: Companies must provide members entitled to attend meetings with a reasonable opportunity to participate. Meetings must be held at a reasonable physical venue, and if held at multiple venues, the main venue must be specified in the notice. Virtual meeting technology must be reasonable and allow members to exercise rights to ask questions and make comments orally and in writing. Quorum requires at least two members present, with proxies counted as one individual per member. Meetings adjourned after 30 minutes without quorum must be rescheduled with specific rules for location and technology.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for meeting venues and virtual technology to ensure member participation, relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border meetings where physical or virtual participation is required.

requirements.text: A company must give members entitled to attend meetings a reasonable opportunity to participate
The meeting must be held at a reasonable physical venue
If held at multiple physical venues, the main venue must be specified in the notice
Virtual meeting technology must be reasonable
Virtual meeting technology must allow members to exercise rights to ask questions and make comments orally and in writing
Quorum requires at least two members present at all times during the meeting
Proxies count as one individual per member
Meetings adjourned after 30 minutes without quorum must be rescheduled

sections.source_quote: Section 249S(1): A company that holds a meeting of its members must give the members entitled to attend the meeting, as a whole, a reasonable opportunity to participate in the meeting.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for meetings

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Proxy appointments and voting rules for company meetings
sections.summary: Companies must send proxy appointment forms or lists to members who request them or are entitled to appoint proxies. Proxy appointments must include specific details such as the company's name, proxy's name, and meetings at which the appointment may be used. Proxies have the same rights as members but may be restricted by company constitutions. Proxies cannot vote on resolutions related to key management remuneration if they are closely related or part of key management unless the appointment specifies voting instructions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs proxy appointments and voting, relevant for DTIAs involving third-party data processing where proxies may access sensitive financial information.

requirements.text: Companies must send proxy appointment forms or lists to members who request them or are entitled to appoint proxies
Proxy appointments must include the company's name, proxy's name, and meetings at which the appointment may be used
Proxies have the same rights as members but may be restricted by company constitutions
Proxies cannot vote on resolutions related to key management remuneration if they are closely related or part of key management unless the appointment specifies voting instructions

sections.source_quote: Section 250B(1): For an appointment of a proxy for a meeting of a company's members to be effective, the following documents must be received by the company at least 48 hours before the meeting: (a) the proxy's appointment; (b) if the appointment is signed, or otherwise authenticated in a manner prescribed by regulations made for the purposes of subsection 250A(1), by the appointor's attorney—the authority under which the appointment was signed or authenticated or a certified copy of the authority.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Proxy voting validity and representative appointments
sections.summary: A proxy vote remains valid even if the appointing member dies, becomes mentally incapacitated, revokes the proxy, or transfers shares. The proxy must specify how they vote on a resolution. A body corporate may appoint a representative to exercise powers at meetings, with restrictions on the representative's authority. Multiple representatives can be appointed but only one may exercise powers at any time. The representative may vote as the body corporate on all matters unless restricted.
sections.dtia_summary: Proxy voting rules ensure valid votes can be cast even if the appointing member changes status, relevant for cross-border voting mechanisms where members may be located internationally.

requirements.text: Proxy vote valid even if proxy cannot vote as member
Proxy must specify how they vote on a resolution
Proxy vote valid even if appointing member dies
Proxy vote valid even if appointing member is mentally incapacitated
Proxy vote valid even if appointing member revokes proxy
Proxy vote valid even if appointing member transfers shares
Body corporate may appoint representative to exercise powers
Representative may exercise all powers at meetings
Only one representative may exercise powers at any time

sections.source_quote: A proxy who is not entitled to vote on a resolution as a member may vote as a proxy for another member who can vote if their appointment specifies the way they are to vote on the resolution and they vote that way.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: AGM requirements and auditor questions
sections.summary: Public companies must hold annual general meetings (AGMs) within 18 months of registration and at least once yearly within 5 months of financial year end. Listed companies must submit written questions to auditors before AGMs, which the company must pass to the auditor. The auditor must prepare a question list for members attending the AGM to review.
sections.dtia_summary: AGM requirements and auditor questions ensure transparency in financial reporting, relevant for data transfers involving financial disclosures across borders.

requirements.text: Public company must hold AGM within 18 months of registration
Public company must hold AGM at least once yearly within 5 months of financial year end
Member may submit written question to auditor relevant to auditor's report
Company must pass question to auditor within 5 business days before AGM
Auditor must prepare question list for members at AGM

sections.source_quote: A member of a listed company who is entitled to cast a vote at the AGM may submit a written question to the auditor under this section if the question is relevant to the content of any auditor's report to be considered at the AGM.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure governance, no data compliance relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Remuneration report voting thresholds for special meetings
sections.summary: This section establishes conditions for holding special meetings when at least 25% of votes oppose remuneration reports at two consecutive annual general meetings. It requires a resolution to hold fresh elections for directors within 90 days if the voting thresholds are met, with specific rules for director appointments and the spill meeting timeline. The company must ensure the spill meeting occurs within 90 days unless directors cease holding office before the deadline, and failure to hold the meeting within this period constitutes an offence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates specific conditions for holding special meetings when remuneration report voting thresholds are met, requiring a 90-day deadline for the spill meeting and imposing strict liability for failure to hold it within the timeframe. These conditions directly impact data transfer impact assessments for companies needing to coordinate meetings across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: At the later AGM, there must be put to vote a resolution that another meeting be held within 90 days
All directors who were directors when the resolution was passed and are not managing directors must cease holding office immediately before the spill meeting
Resolutions to appoint persons to offices vacated by the spill meeting must be put to vote at the spill meeting
The company must hold the spill meeting within 90 days after the spill resolution is passed
If directors cease holding office before the deadline, the company need not hold the spill meeting within 90 days
Failure to hold the spill meeting within 90 days commits each director to an offence of strict liability

sections.source_quote: Section 250V(1) requires a resolution to hold fresh elections for directors at special meeting to be put to vote at AGM within 90 days if voting thresholds are met.
sections.relevance_reason: irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Minutes and members' access to minutes
sections.summary: This section mandates that companies maintain minute books recording meetings, resolutions, and declarations within one month. It requires minutes to be signed by the chair or next meeting chair within a reasonable time, and ensures minute books are accessible to members free of charge. Companies must provide copies within 14 days or as approved by ASIC, with payment capped at a prescribed amount. Failure to comply constitutes an offence of strict liability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs minute-keeping practices that impact data transfer impact assessments by requiring detailed records of member meetings and resolutions, which may be relevant for cross-border data transfers involving corporate governance structures.

requirements.text: A company must keep minute books recording proceedings and resolutions of meetings within 1 month
Minutes of meetings must be signed by the chair or next meeting chair within a reasonable time
Minutes of resolutions without meetings must be signed by a director within a reasonable time
Minute books must be kept at the registered office, principal place of business, or approved location
Companies must ensure minute books are open for inspection by members free of charge
Members may request copies of minutes within 14 days or as approved by ASIC
Payment for copies cannot exceed the prescribed amount

sections.source_quote: Section 251A(1) requires companies to keep minute books recording proceedings and resolutions of meetings within 1 month.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for compliance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Member meeting notices and proxy appointments
sections.summary: A registered scheme must provide members with meeting notices in specific formats, including meeting details, business nature, proxy rights, and electronic communication options. The notice period varies by delivery method, with postal notices taking 3 days and electronic notices effective the next business day. The responsible entity must also distribute member resolutions and statements if requested by at least 100 entitled members, with specific conditions for validity and costs.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for meeting notices and member resolutions, impacting how data is transferred and processed across borders through member communications and proxy mechanisms. It specifies notice formats and delivery timelines that affect data handling practices.

requirements.text: A notice of meeting must set out meeting details, business nature, proxy rights, and electronic communication options
Notice sent by post is taken to be given 3 days after posting
Notice sent electronically is taken to be given on the business day after sending
The responsible entity must distribute member resolutions if requested by at least 100 entitled members
The responsible entity must ensure proxy appointments are valid even if the appointing member dies or revokes the appointment
The responsible entity must pay for distribution costs if the notice is sent with the meeting notice

sections.source_quote: A notice of a meeting of a registered scheme's members must: (a) set out... (ii) if there are 2 or more locations... (iii) if virtual meeting technology is to be used... (b) state the general nature of the meeting's business; (c) if a special or extraordinary resolution is to be proposed... (d) contain a statement setting out the following information: (i) that the member has a right to appoint a proxy; (ii) that the proxy does not need to be a member of the registered scheme; (iii) that if the member appoints 2 proxies the member may specify the proportion or number of votes the proxy is appointed to exercise; and (e) specify at least one of the following: (i) a place for the purposes of receipt of proxy appointments and proxy appointment authorities; (ii) sufficient information to allow members to comply with section 252Z by means of an electronic communication.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for member communications

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer rules

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Electronic minute books and independent observer rights for shareholder meetings
sections.summary: This section requires companies to maintain minute books in electronic form if the information is reasonably accessible for subsequent reference. It also mandates that companies appoint independent persons to observe and report on shareholder meetings when members with at least 5% voting rights request it. Companies must provide reasonable steps to ensure independent observers and report on poll conduct within 5 business days of request. Failure to comply constitutes an offence of strict liability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for electronic minute books and independent observer rights for shareholder meetings, which may impact data transfer assessments when companies store or share minute book information across borders.

requirements.text: If information is required to be recorded in a minute book, it may be recorded in electronic form if reasonably expected to be accessible for subsequent reference
Electronic minute books must be open for inspection at the place where required to be kept
Electronic minute books must provide reliable means of assuring information integrity
Companies must appoint independent persons to observe and report on poll conduct within 5 business days of request
Companies must ensure independent persons observe poll conduct and prepare reports within reasonable time
Companies must pay fees to independent persons appointed for poll observation
Companies must provide information to independent persons within reasonable time after request
Companies must keep copies of reports on poll conduct for compliance

sections.source_quote: Section 253S(1): If information is required to be recorded in a minute book, the information may be recorded in electronic form if, at the time of the recording of the information, it was reasonable to expect that the information would be readily accessible so as to be useable for subsequent reference.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for shareholder meetings

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: regulator powers and enforcement

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Share conversion and redemption rules for companies
sections.summary: Companies may convert ordinary shares to preference shares and vice versa, but must specify repayment of capital, profit participation, dividend terms, voting rights, and priority of payment in their constitution or by special resolution. Redeemable preference shares cannot be converted from non-redeemable shares. Redemption must follow terms of issue, be fully paid-up, and use profits or new share proceeds. Contraventions of redemption rules do not invalidate transactions but may constitute offences.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs share conversion and redemption processes, which are relevant to DTIAs when companies transfer or store share data across borders, particularly for financial institutions handling shareholder information.

requirements.text: A company may convert an ordinary share into a preference share
A company may convert a preference share into an ordinary share

The holders' rights with respect to repayment of capital, participation in surplus assets and profits, cumulative and non-cumulative dividends, voting, and priority of payment must be set out in the company's constitution or approved by special resolution
A company may convert all or any of its shares into a larger or smaller number of shares by resolution passed at a general meeting
The conversion takes effect on the day the resolution is passed or a later date specified in the resolution
Any amount unpaid on shares being converted is to be divided equally among the replacement shares
The company must lodge a copy of the resolution with ASIC within 1 month after it is passed
A company may redeem redeemable preference shares only on the terms on which they are on issue
On redemption, the shares are cancelled
A company may only redeem redeemable preference shares if the shares are fully paid-up
A company may only redeem redeemable preference shares out of profits or the proceeds of a new issue of shares made for the purpose of the redemption
If a company redeems shares in contravention of section 254J or 254K, the contravention does not affect the validity of the redemption or of any contract or transaction connected with it

sections.source_quote: Section 254G(1): A company may: (a) convert an ordinary share into a preference share; and (b) convert a preference share into an ordinary share.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: regulator powers and enforcement

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Share capital reductions and buy-backs: shareholder approval and procedures
sections.summary: This section requires companies to follow specific rules when reducing share capital or buying back shares. The rules ensure fairness to shareholders, protect creditors from insolvency risks, and mandate disclosure of material information. Shareholders must approve reductions through ordinary or special resolutions depending on whether the reduction is equal or selective. Companies must also lodge resolutions with ASIC within 14 days and comply with procedural requirements for buy-backs.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for shareholder approval and procedural compliance in share capital reductions and buy-backs, which may impact data transfers if the company handles financial data or customer information through share transactions.

requirements.text: A company may reduce its share capital in a way not otherwise authorised by law if the reduction is fair and reasonable to shareholders as a whole
The reduction must not materially prejudice the company's ability to pay creditors
The reduction must be approved by shareholders under section 256C
The reduction is an equal reduction if it relates only to ordinary shares and applies proportionally to each holder
The reduction is a selective reduction if it does not apply proportionally to each holder
For equal reductions, shareholders must approve via ordinary resolution
For selective reductions, shareholders must approve via special or unanimous resolution
The company must lodge with ASIC a copy of any resolution within 14 days after passing
The company must not make the reduction until 14 days after lodgment

sections.source_quote: Section 256B(1): A company may reduce its share capital in a way that is not otherwise authorised by law if the reduction: (a) is fair and reasonable to the company's shareholders as a whole; and (b) does not materially prejudice the company's ability to pay its creditors; and (c) is approved by shareholders under section 256C.
sections.relevance_reason: Share capital rules not data compliance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Share buy-back procedures: shareholder approval and regulatory compliance
sections.summary: This section outlines the procedures for share buy-backs, including shareholder approval requirements, disclosure obligations, and ASIC lodgments. Buy-backs must comply with specific limits on share acquisition (10/12 rule), require shareholder approval depending on the type of buy-back, and involve detailed documentation for ASIC. The rules ensure transparency and prevent conflicts of interest in share transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies procedural requirements for share buy-backs that may affect data transfers if the company handles financial data or customer information through share transactions.

requirements.text: The company must satisfy the lodgment requirement with ASIC at least 14 days before the resolution is passed or the agreement is entered into
The company must include with the offer a statement of all material information known to the company
The company must not dispose of shares it buys back; agreements in contravention are void
Shares bought back must be cancelled immediately after registration
The 10/12 limit for buy-backs is 10% of the smallest number of votes attaching to voting shares over the last 12 months
If the 10/12 limit is exceeded, the buy-back requires special or unanimous shareholder approval

sections.source_quote: Section 257F(1): If section 257B applies this section to a buy-back, the company must satisfy the lodgment requirement in subsection (2) at least 14 days before: (a) if the buy-back agreement is conditional on the passing of a resolution under subsection 257C(1) or 257D(1)—the resolution is passed; or (b) if it is not—the agreement is entered into.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial regulation, no data privacy or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Share capital reductions and cancellations for companies
sections.summary: This section authorizes share capital reductions from redemption of redeemable preference shares using new share issues, buying back shares with share capital, and cancellations under specific sections. It also permits reductions due to court orders. The section ensures these reductions are valid and authorized by the company.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines conditions for valid share capital reductions, which may impact data transfers if companies adjust share structures or hold data in share-related entities.

requirements.text: Any reduction in share capital involved in redemption of redeemable preference shares out of proceeds of new share issues for redemption is authorized
Any reduction in share capital from buying back shares with share capital is authorized
A company may cancel shares returned under specified sections and any share capital reduction is authorized
Any reduction in share capital due to an order under section 1325A is authorized

sections.source_quote: Any reduction in share capital involved in: (a) the redemption of redeemable preference shares out of the proceeds of a new issue of shares made for the purpose of the redemption (see section 254K); or (b) a company's buying-back of its own shares under sections 257A to 257J if the shares are paid for out of share capital.
sections.relevance_reason: Share capital reductions irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: [sections 461—462] (cont.)
sections.title: Financial assistance for share acquisitions and exemptions
sections.summary: This section details financial assistance rules for acquiring shares, requiring shareholder approval, exemptions for ordinary commercial dealings, financial institutions, and subsidiaries of debenture issuers. It mandates specific approvals and documentation for financial assistance to ensure it does not prejudice company interests or creditors.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs financial assistance for share acquisitions, which may affect data transfers if companies use financial assistance to acquire shares or related entities holding data.

requirements.text: Financial assistance for share acquisitions must not materially prejudice company interests or ability to pay creditors
Financial assistance must be approved by shareholders under section 260B
Financial assistance may be given before or after share acquisition
Financial assistance may take the form of paying a dividend
Financial assistance is exempted if given in ordinary course of commercial dealing for partly-paid shares
Financial assistance is exempted if provided by financial institutions in ordinary course of business
Financial assistance is exempted for subsidiaries of debenture issuers under specific conditions

sections.source_quote: A company may financially assist a person to acquire shares (or units of shares) in the company or a holding company of the company only if: (a) giving the assistance does not materially prejudice: (i) the interests of the company or its shareholders; or (ii) the company's ability to pay its creditors; or (b) the assistance is approved by shareholders under section 260B (that section also requires advance notice to ASIC); or (c) the assistance is exempted under section 2: 260C.
sections.relevance_reason: No data privacy or cross-border transfer relevance
